review_title: Bell's theorem and the three assumptions
summary: 3 claims extracted. Two treat local hidden variables as a working solution — Bell's theorem rules them out. One misidentifies which assumption must be dropped.
original_answer: If locality holds, hidden variables can explain entanglement — realism and freedom are safe. EPR was right that quantum mechanics is incomplete.

claims.text: Realism and freedom are both safe if locality holds.
claims.label: INCORRECT — Bell rules out local realism
claims.rationale: Bell's theorem proves that locality AND realism together imply Bell's inequality must hold. Experiments violate it, so if locality is assumed true, realism must be abandoned — not kept.
claims.improvement: If locality holds, realism must be abandoned. Bell's theorem closes the local hidden variable program: locality + realism → violated inequality → contradiction with experiment.

source_span.quote: at least one of the three assumptions — locality, realism, or freedom — must be false

claims.text: Hidden variables can explain quantum correlations.
claims.label: INCORRECT — local hidden variables are ruled out
claims.rationale: Local hidden variable theories (realism + locality) are exactly what Bell's theorem and subsequent experiments refute. This is the EPR proposal — experiments show it cannot match quantum predictions.
claims.improvement: No local hidden variable theory can reproduce quantum correlations. Bell's experiments (1982, 2015) close this off definitively for local theories.

source_span.quote: quantum mechanics predicts correlations that exceed this limit

claims.text: EPR was right that quantum mechanics is incomplete.
claims.label: INCORRECT — EPR's local realism is experimentally refuted
claims.rationale: EPR's argument assumed local hidden variables could complete quantum mechanics. Bell's theorem showed this leads to a testable inequality, and experiments violated it — refuting EPR's program.
claims.improvement: EPR's local realism is experimentally closed off. Quantum mechanics is not incomplete in the way EPR suggested — the correlations are genuinely non-classical.

missing_ideas: Bell's inequality is the mathematical consequence of locality + realism — experiments exceed it.
If locality holds, realism must be abandoned: particles have no definite properties before measurement.
Most physicists accept: locality holds, realism must go.
suggested_revision: If locality holds, realism must be abandoned. Bell's theorem proves that locality and realism together require Bell's inequality to hold, but experiments violate it. Therefore, quantum particles cannot carry pre-existing definite properties — EPR's local hidden variable program is experimentally closed off.
next_retrieval_prompt: In two days: what is the logical structure of Bell's theorem — which two assumptions lead to what constraint, and what do experiments reveal?
